mnemonics.title: AGILE VALUES MEMORY HOOK
mnemonics.content: REMEMBER THE FOUR VALUES: 'I WORK COLLABORATIVELY' - I (Individuals/interactions), W (Working software), C (Customer collaboration), R (Responding to change). This forms I-W-C-R to recall all four values.

mnemonics.title: AGILE PRINCIPLES MEMORY HOOK
mnemonics.content: CUSTOMER FIRST: 'CUSTOMER SATISFACTION WELCOMES CHANGES, FREQUENT DELIVERY, DAILY COLLABORATION, MOTIVATED TEAMS, FACE-TO-FACE TALK, WORKING SOFTWARE MEASURES PROGRESS, SUSTAINABLE PACE, TECHNICAL EXCELLENCE, SIMPLICITY, SELF-ORGANIZING TEAMS, REGULAR REFLECTION' - Each word starts with letters C through R (12 principles).

acronyms.usage: Describes the adaptive nature of agile methodology focusing on individuals, lean practices, and efficiency in software development.
The smallest thing you can build that delivers customer value - used in agile to get early feedback.

acronyms.expansion: Short, time-boxed iteration for delivering incremental value
acronyms.usage: A fixed time period (usually 1-4 weeks) during which a specific set of work must be completed.
A popular agile framework with roles (Product Owner, Scrum Master, Development Team) and ceremonies (Sprint Planning, Daily Standup, Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective).

visualTips.visualization: Picture a spiral growing outward with each loop representing an iteration that adds new features while building on previous work. Each loop produces a more complete product.
Draw a flat or slightly rising curve (agile) vs an exponential curve (waterfall) showing how agility keeps change costs low throughout development.

Think of a pyramid with VALUES at the top (what matters most), PRINCIPLES in the middle (guidelines), and PRACTICES at the base (specific techniques). Values override principles, principles override practices.
Imagine a circle of team members with arrows pointing inward toward a central product, all equally contributing rather than a hierarchical structure with a manager at top.

analogies.analogy: Like building a house room by room rather than completing all architectural drawings first. You live in each room, get feedback, and adjust the next room's design based on how the first room works.
Like navigating with a GPS that continuously recalculates the route based on traffic rather than following a fixed map from the start.
Like a chef cooking with the customer present at the table, adjusting the dish in real-time based on their reactions and feedback rather than cooking in a closed kitchen.
Like a prototype car that actually drives rather than just having detailed blueprints - you can test the actual product, not just documentation about it.
